exemplar.title: Creating an interactive mathematics game for my client (middle school math tutor)

detailedExemplarData.title: Creating an interactive mathematics game for my client (middle school math tutor)

strengths: Clear identification of scenario and two client roles
Well-justified rationale linking system features to client needs
Comprehensive and extensive success criteria covering all major functions
weaknesses: Evidence of client consultation is indirect and lacks dated notes or transcripts
Rationale remains qualitative without comparison to other solutions
A few success criteria are phrased vaguely and could be more measurable
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the planning is solid: the scenario is clearly defined with evidence of at least one round of client discussion, the rationale is coherent and tied to client requirements, and the success criteria cover a comprehensive range of functionality. However, the consultation evidence is not fully detailed, and the rationale could have been strengthened by comparing alternatives. On balance this merits near-top marks (5/6).

strengths: System handles role-based authentication, dynamic trophy logic and analyticsâshowing strong complexity
Appropriate and effective use of a DBMS and GUI-building tools
Robust flowchart and UML class diagram illustrate deep understanding of system processes
weaknesses: Documentation of external sources and techniques is minimal, no bibliography provided
Mock-ups omit certain error and loading states, reducing clarity of the full UX flow
Class diagram lacks cardinalities and the structure diagram omits data-flow arrows
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development work demonstrates a high level of complexity and an effective use of tools, with strong system modelling artifacts. Only the documentation of sources and some UX mock-up details are missing. This places the work just shy of a perfect 12âan 11/12 is warranted given the overall ingenuity and tool use.

strengths: Highly detailed and systematic test plan linked to success criteria
Comprehensive design overview with multiple complementary diagrams
Mock-ups and UML diagrams give clear insight into data structures and navigation
weaknesses: No formal or dated task list to show chronological development
Formatting of the test plan is uneven and a few expected results are blank
Record of development milestones is minimal
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution overview excels in design and testing detailâtwo strands are clearly in the top bandâbut the absence of a structured, dated task log pulls the criterion just below the maximum. A holistic score of 5/6 reflects these strengths and that one area remains weak. The lack of a clear task record prevents a perfect mark.

strengths: Architectural design makes expansion theoretically possible
Mock-ups clearly separate educator and student functions
Screens for performance reporting are well organized
weaknesses: No running prototype or video, so actual functionality cannot be verified
Hard-coded thresholds in design make schema changes non-trivial
UX designs lack pagination/filters for large datasets
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design strongly suggests full functionality and straightforward extensibility, the absence of a live demonstration limits confirmed performance. The work sits at the top of the mid band for this criterion (4/4 would be maximum simplicity-based marks), but since actual functionality isnât proven, it earns 4/4 of the available mid-band points (interpreted as 4/4 on the scale shown).

strengths: Makes some tentative recommendations about topic suggestions and grade improvement potential
weaknesses: Recommendations are hypothetical and not well justified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation is very limited: there is no formal post-implementation reflection, no client feedback, and only trivial, hypothetical recommendations. This aligns with the lower band descriptors and merits 2/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple complementary diagrams are included: a detailed flow-chart, a UML class diagram with attributes/methods, a structure diagram and extensive screen mock-ups annotated with validation notes.  Together they give a clear picture of navigation, data structures and user interaction, satisfying the requirement for a comprehensive design overview.

The student explains why a bespoke quiz-management system is preferable to existing generic tools: it stores per-student progress, awards trophies, lets teachers upload graded questions, and motivates engagement.  These benefits are linked to the clientâs need to track topic mastery.  The explanation is coherent but remains largely qualitative and lacks comparison with alternative approaches or technical constraints, so it is a partial rather than full justification.

Database tables with keys, data-type constraints and UI mock-ups imply use of a DBMS, GUI builder and server-side code.  These tools are appropriate and used in more than a trivial manner, but the submission does not evidence advanced features such as automated testing pipelines or ORM, so the upper half of the middle band is appropriate.

The table-based test plan lists many functional areas, links each to success-criterion numbers, describes methods and expected outcomes.  While formatting is uneven and a few expected-result cells are blank, overall coverage is detailed and systematic, placing it in the top band.

The submission briefly mentions that grades could improve and that teachers might suggest topics, but this is hypothetical and not linked to concrete next steps.  Recommendations are therefore trivial and only loosely realistic.

The student cites no external libraries, tutorials or datasets, and there is no bibliography.  Technique explanations are surface-level (e.g., "Compare password with database").  Some in-text descriptions of SQL constraints show partial documentation, placing this strand in the lower band.

No chronological task list is provided.  At best, scattered headings such as âFile 1â and âCriteria Bâ imply milestones, but there is no evidence of dates, responsibilities or progress notes.  This fits a limited record that poorly demonstrates the development process.

The proposed product handles multi-user authentication, role-based menus, real-time performance tracking, trophy logic, CRUD for questions, and progress analyticsâfeatures that require relational databases, conditional logic and aggregation.  This represents a moderate (but not exceptional) level of complexity and ingenuity.

Thirteen numbered items are stated in the test plan and cover validation, login security, reporting, incentive allocation, topic progress, timing, menu navigation etc., together forming a broad set of measurable outcomes.  Although a few criteria are vague (e.g., âIt should be some positive valueâ), the list is extensive and generally appropriate, meeting the descriptor for a comprehensive range.
No email, interview notes, or surveys from the client/adviser after development are provided; therefore there is no evidence of feedback.

Database-centred architecture would theoretically allow future tables and menu items to be added, but the design ties many queries to fixed field names and hard-coded award thresholds.  Expansion is possible yet would require non-trivial schema alterations.  This aligns with âexpansion and modification possible but difficultâ.

The student identifies a clear educational-game scenario aimed at mathematics revision and explicitly names two client roles (teacher and student). Screenshots of interface mock-ups and a list of required features show that at least one round of discussion with the intended users has taken place (e.g., incentive rules of 8/10 = trophy came "from client").  However, evidence of consultation is limited to indirect references; there are no dated meeting notes, interview transcripts, or quotations.  The description is therefore better than a simple statement but does not reach the âdetailed evidence of consultationâ expected for the top band.

Because only design prototypes are provided and no demonstration video or evidence of a running system is included, the assessor can only assume partial functionality.  Screens and logic are planned but not proven to work, fitting the descriptor "product functions partially as demonstrated in video" at best; therefore minimum marks of the middle band are awarded.
An evaluation section is absent.  The only reflection is implicit in the test plan, which is not post-implementation.  Hence there is merely a limited attempt to consider success criteria.

annotations.comment: The submenu for âPlay Gameâ is well organized, grouping related options logically.
The flowchart provides a comprehensive overview of the systemâs processes, showing user actions and decision points clearly.
The flowchart lacks a standardized start/end symbol and legend, which can confuse readers about entry and exit points.
Consider adding a legend for symbols and explicitly marking the start and end nodes to improve the flowchartâs readability.
Add mock-ups for key error conditions (e.g., duplicate username) and specify layout adaptations for mobile screens.
The login screen mock-up clearly identifies input fields and navigation elements, supporting usability.
Failure and loading states are not represented, leaving ambiguity about user feedback during authentication.
Include mock-ups showing invalid credential messages and spinner animations to capture a complete UX flow.
The UML class diagram lists attributes and methods thoroughly, demonstrating careful consideration of system functionality.
Relationships and cardinalities between classes are missing, making it hard to see how entities interact within the database.
Add association lines with multiplicities (e.g., 1-to-many) to show cardinality and strengthen the modelâs clarity.
The structure diagram effectively decomposes the system into modules, clarifying high-level organization.
Data flow arrows are not shown between components, which reduces visibility of inter-module interactions.
Consider annotating the diagram with data flow arrows or numbering to show how information moves between modules.
The sign-up prototype specifies field validation rules clearly, aiding future implementation.
The mock-up omits examples of error states (e.g., invalid email or mismatched passwords), which are crucial for UX design.
The incentives screen mock-up shows rewards but lacks demonstration of how counters dynamically update.
Illustrate real-time updates to trophies and certificates in the incentives mock-up to reflect system behavior.
The login history table displays comprehensive fields for auditing user sessions.
No filtering or pagination controls are shown, which could limit usability when history records grow.
Propose adding search filters and pagination to the history screen to maintain performance as data scales.
The teacher main menu mock-up clearly differentiates educator functions from student views.
Menu items are not annotated with required permission levels, risking ambiguity in access control.
Annotate which menu options require teacher privileges versus those open to all users.
The quiz details screen presents all question properties, including hints and level, in a clear layout.
The questions list lacks sorting or filtering controls, which would improve the userâs ability to find specific items.
Incorporate dropdown filters by topic or difficulty and allow column sorting to enhance manageability.
The specific question view neatly displays answer keys and hints, aiding teachers in evaluation.
Hint text is not visually distinguished, which may confuse users about its purpose versus main content.
Use distinct styling (e.g., italics or color) for hint sections to improve readability and user guidance.
The performance screen mock-up succinctly aggregates student scores and progress.
No graph visualizations or trend lines are shown, missing an opportunity to depict performance over time.
Include a mock-up of a chart (e.g., line graph) to illustrate performance trends over multiple sessions.
Consider adding an export button to allow teachers to download performance data for offline analysis.
